main_ingredients: ground spices (cumin, coriander, garam masala, chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Bharwan Baingan Ke Kabab consists of 2 to 3 stuffed aubergine halves, weighing approximately 200 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Brinjal (cooked), Paneer, Potato (boiled) composite estimation; ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Bharwan Baingan Ke Kabab as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data: brinjal (cooked, ~50% by weight), paneer (~25% by weight), boiled potato (~20% by weight), and ground spices/oil (~5% by weight). Weighted average applied. Tandoor cooking method assumed to reduce moisture and concentrate nutrients slightly relative to boiled/steamed values. Values represent a reasonable mid-range estimate for this dish category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable and paneer dishes — analogous estimate based on eggplant (GI ~15), potato (boiled, GI ~78), and paneer (GI ~27) weighted composite; University of Sydney GI Database reference values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bharwan Baingan Ke Kabab. GI estimated as weighted composite: aubergine/brinjal (GI ~15, low, University of Sydney GI Database), boiled potato (GI ~78, high, University of Sydney GI Database), paneer (GI ~27, low, University of Sydney GI Database), ground spices (negligible carbohydrate contribution). Approximate weight fractions: brinjal 50%, potato 20%, paneer 25%, spices 5%. Weighted GI ≈ (0.50×15) + (0.20×78) + (0.25×27) + (0.05×5) ≈ 7.5 + 15.6 + 6.75 + 0.25 ≈ 30. Adjusted upward to ~45 to account for tandoor cooking effect on starch gelatinisation in potato component and realistic restaurant portion composition. GL per 200 g serving = (45 × 10.2 × 2.0) / 100 ≈ 9.2. Classified as low GI (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spice blends and cooking process
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 420 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation, as cumulative sodium from spiced restaurant dishes can contribute meaningfully to the 1500–2000 mg daily target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated glycemic index (~45) driven by high aubergine and paneer content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low estimated GI (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (~10 g per 100 g) make this dish a relatively favourable choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes, though the potato component should be noted and portion size kept to one standard serving (200 g) to limit glycemic load to approximately 9 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from paneer and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Paneer contributes approximately 3.9 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and frequency of consumption, and note that tandoor cooking without added oil is preferable to pan-fried variants of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bharwan Baingan Ke Kabab is a North Indian tandoor-cooked dish in which whole aubergines (brinjal/eggplant) are scooped out and stuffed with a spiced mixture of paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese), boiled potato, and aromatic ground spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, and green chilli, then skewered and cooked in a clay tandoor oven at high heat. The tandoor imparts a characteristic smoky char to the aubergine skin while keeping the interior moist and flavourful. The texture contrasts the soft, slightly smoky aubergine shell with the creamy, spiced paneer-potato filling. This dish is vegetarian and gluten-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary preferences. It is classified as a house special at several North Indian restaurants in Singapore, including Curry Village (Boat Quay), Bombay Grill, Bombay Curry and Kebabs, and Aryana. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~118 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from paneer (~5.2 g per 100 g), and dietary fibre from aubergine (~2.4 g per 100 g). The estimated glycemic index is low (~45), making it a relatively blood-glucose-friendly option compared to bread-based or rice-based starters. Sodium content is moderate at ~210 mg per 100 g, primarily from spice blends. The dish is relevant for clinical dietary counselling in the context of Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, moderate carbohydrate), hypertension (moderate sodium), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from paneer warrants portion awareness). It is served as a starter or appetiser in North Indian restaurant settings.

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or USDA entry exists for Bharwan Baingan Ke Kabab as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN 2017 component data for brinjal (cooked), paneer, and boiled potato with weighted averaging. GI is an analogous composite estimate using University of Sydney GI Database reference values for individual components. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates derived from ICMR-NIN 2017 data for brinjal (cooked), paneer, and boiled potato; no single approved-source entry for this composite dish exists. 2. GI is an analogous composite estimate; no direct GI measurement for Bharwan Baingan Ke Kabab was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 for these component foods. 4. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN 2017 for these component foods. 5. Serving size of 200 g is an estimate based on typical North Indian restaurant starter portions; actual restaurant portions may vary between 150–250 g. 6. Sodium estimate of 210 mg per 100 g includes contribution from spice blends and any added salt during preparation; actual sodium may be higher in restaurant preparations. 7. The dish is confirmed vegetarian (contains dairy/paneer) and is not vegan. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the vegetarian nature of the dish (no meat); individual restaurant certification should be verified. 9. FoodOn match FOODON_03302757 is a closest_parent match; no exact FoodOn term for tandoori stuffed aubergine kebab was identified. 10. FoodEx2 code A0FMT (aubergine, cooked) is used as the closest_parent anchor; this is an analogous estimate.
